item.title: Optimization and Analysis of Smooth Functions
item.description:
This is the L-Lipschitz continuity of the gradient, i.e., the function f is L-smooth.

References:

Descent Lemma Smoothness bound

Dartmouth Lecture Notes on smooth functions

Stanford’s C-ME 323 Lecture 8

Francis Bach’s Convex Optimization Notes

“Convex Optimization” by Boyd & Vandenberghe – see the “Descent Lemma”.

“First-Order Methods in Optimization” by Amir Beck.

“Introductory Lectures on Convex Optimization” by Nesterov.

You’ll find this exact exercise in most advanced calculus or optimization courses

item.title: Calculus Preview
item.description:
A great discovery solves a great problem but there is a grain of discovery in the solution of any problem. Your problem may be modest; but if it challenges your curiosity and brings into play your inventive faculties, and if you solve it by your own means, you may experience the tension and enjoy the triumph of discovery.

Focus on conceptual understanding.

The art of teaching, is the art of assisting discovery. I have tried to write a book that assists students in discovering calculus – both for its practical power and its surprising beauty.

item.title: First Taylor Approximation
item.description:
Also known as the linear approximation.

The first-order Taylor approximation of a function provides a linear approximation to the function near a specific point. It is based on the first two terms of the Taylor series expansion.

This measures the error between the actual change in a function and the first-order Taylor approximation of that change.

Gradient (transposed to a row vector) of the function at θ. Linear (first-order) approximation of the change in f.

item.title: Lipschitz Continuous
item.description:
The terms “Lipschitz constraint” and “Lipschitz continuous” are closely related, and often used in the context of optimization, differential equations, and machine learning.

Lipschitz continuous. A function is Lipschitz continuous if there exists a constant L>0 such that This means the function does not change faster than linearly in its inputs.

Lipschitz constraint. The Lipschitz constraint typically refers to imposing a Lipschitz condition, often in optimization or learning tasks. Enforcing that a function (or its gradient) is Lipschitz continuous with a known constant L. This is also called L-smoothness.

item.title: Directional Derivatives
item.description:
Travel east from: 从…向东走

Directional derivative enables us to find the rate of change of a function of two or more variables in any direction.

item.title: second-order linear differential equations
item.description:
solve problems concerning the vibrations of springs and the analysis of electric circuits.

How infinite series can be used to solve differential equations.
